data.title: Whitestone REIT: Undervalued Gem With Double-Digit Rent Spreads
data.description:
Underappreciated gems can be found in many parts of the market, so long as one is willing to do a bit of digging. After all, most of them start small like Realty Income (O), which began with just one humble Taco Bell store. This brings me to Whitestone REIT (NYSE:WSR), which I last covered in January, highlighting its strong lease spreads and attractive property profile across the growing Sunbelt markets.

It appears the market hasn’t yet agreed with my thesis, as the stock has declined by 3% since that piece (-2% total return including dividends), which still far outperformed the 11% decline in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe. Despite the recent drop in price, WSR is still up by 19.5% over the past 12 months, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit WSR including recent business fundamentals and outlook and discuss why the recent dip presents a great buying opportunity for value and growth investors alike, so let’s dive in!

Why WSR?

Whitestone REIT is a shopping center REIT that focuses on open air properties in high-growth Sunbelt markets like Phoenix, Houston, Austin and San Antonio. What differentiates WSR from its peers is its high focus on small shops, which represent 77% of its annual base rent. Notably, 100% of WSR’s rent comes from the low regulation states of Texas and Arizona.

Small shops provide greater leasing flexibility due to their fungible nature (as opposed to big box stores) and have lower capital intensity while charging higher rents per square foot. It also has 100% triple net leases, which allow for recover of over 90% of CAM (common area maintenance), tax and insurance costs. Over the past 4 years, WSR has demonstrated occupancy growth and strong leasing spreads, with 11 consecutive quarters of +17% lease spreads as shown below.

WSR demonstrated robust financial performance during 2024 with Core FFO per share growing by 11% YoY to $1.01. Since 2021, WSR has seen an FFO/share CAGR of 5.5%. Occupancy remained steady on a YoY basis at 94.1%, down just 10 basis points compared to the prior year. At the same time, WSR had strong pricing power and proactive leasing, which drove an impressive 36.1% lease spread on new leases and 19% on renewal leases. These factors contributed to same-store NOI growth of 5.1% for the full year.

Beyond attractive rent spreads on new and renewal leases, WSR also benefits from contractual escalators averaging 2.3% annually. This, combined with a weighted average lease term of 4.1 years means that WSR gets the benefit of both worlds, in the form of annual rent bumps and faster potential for mark-to-market rents. This results in better inflation protection compared to net lease REITs, which have annual rent bumps but longer lease terms and other shopping center REITs, which generally come with short lease terms but no annual rent bumps.

Management is guiding for long-term Core FFO per share growth of 5% to 7% annually. This is to be driven by continued operational growth and redevelopment activities, which are expected to accelerate this year, adding to NOI growth next year and beyond. This includes redevelopment initiatives such as transformative projects at Williams Trace and Lion Square in Houston, as noted during the last earnings call:

One of the more exciting of these redevelopments is already a good portion of the way through the process. That is our Williams Trace Center in Houston. Our improvements to that center and the replacement of an underperforming grocer within EOS fitness has boosted center traffic by 60%. And now with the new focus on leasing activity, we target businesses for an active and upwardly mobile demographic new to that community as well, and we're driving returns from an increased traffic to the center.

Another center where the evolution of community around the center is driving change is Lion Square, which is the heart of the Asian community in Houston. This community is thriving, has almost no occupancy available anywhere near our center. We have the opportunity to modernize the center and transform it with a strong traffic of 18 hours a day. We'll do this at the same time additional development is going on around the center, but importantly we can do it efficiently without interrupting the center's cash flow.

Importantly, WSR has made great strides in improving its balance sheet, with a 2.6x reduction in its debt-to-EBITDAre ratio since 2021 to 6.6x. Management aims for a long-term leverage ratio in the low-6x to high-5x range, which is a reasonably safe level for a REIT. Alongside deleveraging its balance sheet, WSR has also grown its dividend at a 6.5% CAGR since 2021 including 9.1% this year. This is after WSR cut the dividend in 2020 to focus on deleveraging. Notably, WSR pays a monthly dividend, and it’s well-covered by a 51% payout ratio.

Lastly, I continue to find value in WSR at the current price of $13.10 with a forward P/FFO of 12.3. While WSR isn’t the cheapest shopping center REIT out there, I believe its valuation is well worth it considering its robust growth outlook of 5-7% annual FFO/share growth. I also believe WSR could reasonably sustain at least a 5% dividend yield after further reducing its leverage ratio. This means that on a combined basis between the dividend and annual FFO/share growth, WSR could deliver potential total return in the low teens annually.

Risks to WSR include its high exposure to small shops, which carry higher profitability for WSR but can also be more vulnerable to economic downturns compared to big grocers that are essential in nature. Moreover, WSR’s shorter lease term of 4.1 years compared to the 5-year average for shopping center REITs and 10 years for net lease REITs means that WSR carries higher turnover risk. Plus, shopping centers have enjoyed low new supply in the Sunbelt, a ramp up in supply due to lower interest rates could be a headwind for future rent growth.

Investor Takeaway

Whitestone REIT presents a compelling opportunity for value and growth investors, especially after the recent dip in share price. WSR has delivered solid fundamentals. This includes double-digit FFO/share growth, strong lease spreads, and consistent occupancy in high-growth Sunbelt markets.

WSR’s focus on small shops and triple net leases supports high margins and pricing power, while redevelopment initiatives like Williams Trace and Lion Square offer meaningful upside potential. It also pays a well-covered monthly dividend and has an improving balance sheet. With a guided long-term FFO/share growth of 5-7%, WSR is well-positioned to deliver potentially strong total returns in the low teens. As such, I find WSR to be an underappreciated gem in the REIT space.

data.title: XNTK: Technology Dashboard For April
data.description:
This monthly article series reports industry metrics in the information technology sector, focusing on value, quality and momentum. It may also serve as a top-down analysis of sector ETFs like Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK) and SPDR® NYSE Technology ETF (NYSEARCA:XNTK), whose holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non-available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for hardware in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in hardware companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios ((EY, SY, FY)) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios ((ROE, GM)) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

Current data

The next table shows the metrics and scores as of writing. Columns stand for all the data defined above.

Value and Quality chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by industry (higher is better).

Evolution since last month

Value scores have materially improved due to price action in the semiconductors and electronic equipment subsectors. The quality score fell in hardware.

Momentum

The next chart plots momentum scores based on median returns.

Interpretation

The electronic equipment industry is significantly undervalued based on 11-year averages, but its quality score is below the historical baseline. From a fundamental point of view, the least compelling subsectors are hardware (with the worst quality score) and IT services (with the worst value score).

Fast facts on XNTK

SPDR® NYSE Technology ETF was launched on 9/25/2000 and tracks the NYSE Technology Index. It has a portfolio of 35 stocks and an expense ratio of 0.35%, whereas the capital-weighted ETF XLK charges only 0.09% . As described by SSGA, the index is

composed of 35 leading U.S.-listed technology-related companies. (…) Stocks must also meet at least one of the following three revenue- and sales-based criteria: (i) have an increase in sales over the last twelve months, (ii) have only one consecutive quarter of negative sales growth over the last two years, or (iii) have revenue totals from the last four quarters that classify it within the top 75 companies within the specific industry classification (…) The Index is equal-weighted and rebalanced annually.

The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 34.9% of asset value. These are the constituents with the highest price return since the most recent rebalancing. Positions are in equal weight after every annual rebalancing, but they may drift with price action: current weights are between 1.9% (Tesla) and 4.3% (Alibaba Group). The portfolio is more diversified than capital weighted ETFs like XLK, whose three largest positions (Apple, Microsoft and Nvidia) are between 13% and 15%.

Over the last 10 years, XNTK has significantly underperformed XLK and is shortly behind another equal-weight technology ETF: Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Technology ETF (RSPT).

However, XNTK is the best performer over the last 12 months:

In summary, XNTK is an equal-weight technology ETF competing with RSPT as a balanced technology fund with limited exposure to mega-cap companies. Their rebalancing schedules differ: annual for XNTK, quarterly for RSPT. XNTK has a slightly cheaper expense ratio (0.35% vs. 0.40%). On the downside, it also shows lower dollar trading volumes, with respectively $5.43M and $16.22M in daily average.

Dashboard List

I use the first table to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells us that an IT services company with an earnings yield above 0.0321 (or price/earnings below 31.15) is in the better half of the industry regarding this metric. A Dashboard List is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers, with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. The stocks below are part of the list sent to subscribers a few weeks ago.

NATL

NCR Atleos Corp.

IT

Gartner, Inc.

NXPI

NXP Semiconductors N.V.

TDC

Teradata Corp.

YOU

Clear Secure, Inc.

BL

BlackLine, Inc.

It is a rotational model with a statistical bias toward excess returns on the long-term, not the result of an analysis of each stock.

data.title: Western Asset GSM 7-Year Portfolios Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

  • Economic uncertainties triggered a flight to quality during the first quarter of 2025, driving U.S. Treasury yields lower and their prices higher. The economy continued to expand, while inflation remained higher than the Federal Reserve’s (“Fed”) 2% target. Meanwhile, President Trump’s plans for new reciprocal tariffs on U.S. imports led to concerns of rising inflation. All told, the overall U.S. bond market, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index, returned 2.78% during the first quarter. In contrast, the municipal (“muni”) market modestly declined over the quarter.

Quarterly Key Performance Drivers

Allocation

Security Selection

HELPED

Yield curve positioning

HURT

Duration positioning
  • Having a duration that was slightly higher or neutral versus the benchmark detracted from returns.
  • Yield curve positioning was beneficial, as the curve began to steepen.
  • Sector allocation and security selection did not meaningfully impact the strategy’s relative performance during the quarter.

Outlook & Strategy

  • The disinflationary trend may be interrupted as tariffs and retaliatory actions are implemented, but we expect inflation to move lower again over the longer term. Monetary policy remains restrictive. We expect central banks will continue to cut rates modestly in 2025. The Fed remains well positioned to provide support if the U.S. economy falters. Public debt levels continue to rise and yield curves may steepen given concerns over fiscal policies globally.
  • We see pockets of opportunity in developed market rates in Europe, the UK and Australia. While the overall uncertainty in the market environment necessitates caution, we do see some longer-term value opportunities in emerging market local currency debt.
  • Spread sector fundamentals remain supportive, but valuations reflect those fundamentals and credit spreads persist at below historical averages. We continue to find opportunities within spread sectors and related securities, while remaining tactical.
  • Developed market duration provides useful diversification.

The strategy returns shown are preliminary composite returns, subject to future revision (downward or upward). Please visit www.franklintempleton.com for the latest performance figures. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. An investment in this strategy can lose value.

Performance data represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. Investment return and principal value will fluctuate with market conditions, and you may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares. Periods less than one year are not annualized. Performance results are for the composite which includes all actual, fully discretionary accounts with substantially similar investment policies and objectives managed to the composite’s investment strategy. Composite returns are stated in U.S. dollars and assume reinvestment of any dividends, interest income, capital gains, or other earnings. The composite may include account(s) that are gross of fees and pure gross of fees. “Pure” gross-of-fee returns do not reflect the deduction of any expenses, including transaction costs. A traditional (or “true”) gross-of-fee return reflects performance after the reduction of transaction costs but before the reduction of the investment advisory fee. The gross-of-fee return may include a blend of “true” gross-of-fee returns for non-wrap accounts and “pure” gross-of-fee returns for wrap accounts. Net-of-fee returns is reduced by a model “wrap fee” (1.5% is the maximum anticipated wrap fee for fixed income portfolios) which includes trading expenses as well as investment management, administrative and custodial fees. The model wrap fee used represents the highest anticipated wrap fee applicable to the strategy. Actual fees and account minimums may vary.

The Bloomberg U.S. Intermediate Treasury Index is composed of all public obligations of the U.S. Treasury. This index has an average current maturity of3.69 years (average maturity may vary over time). Source: Bloomberg Indices.

Investment Team

Western Asset is one of the world’s leading active fixed-income managers, delivering long-term fundamental value investment solutions to clients for over half a century. Founded in 1971, Western Asset offers clients deep expertise across the range of global fixed-income sectors from nine offices located in key financial markets.

Glossary of Terms

The Federal Reserve (“Fed”) is responsible for the formulation of policies designed to promote economic growth, full employment, stable prices, and a sustainable pattern of international trade and payments.

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index is comprised of investment-grade, U.S. dollar-denominated government, corporate, and mortgage- and asset- backed issues having at least one year to maturity. Source: Bloomberg Indices.

The yield curve shows the relationship between yields and maturity dates for a similar class of bonds.

Important Information

The information contained in this piece is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding the market and any industry, sector, security or portfolio. Statements of fact cited by the manager have been obtained from sources considered reliable but no representation is made as to their completeness or accuracy. Because market and economic conditions are subject to rapid change, opinions provided are valid only as of the date of the material, and are subject to change without notice. The manager’s opinions are intended solely to provide insight into how the manager analyzes securities, may differ from that of other affiliated managers, and are not a recommendation or individual investment advice for any particular security, strategy or investment product. Any securities discussed may not represent an account’s entire portfolio and in the aggregate may represent a small percentage of an account’s portfolio holdings. There is no assurance that any such securities will remain in an account’s portfolio, or that securities sold have not been repurchased. It should not be assumed that any securities transactions discussed were or will prove to be profitable. The information provided should not be considered a recommendation to purchase, sell or hold any particular security. All indexes are unmanaged and cannot accommodate direct investment. Investors should review their investment objectives, risk tolerance and liquidity needs before choosing a manager.

There is no guarantee that investment strategies will work under all market conditions, and investors should evaluate their ability to invest for the long term, especially during periods of market downturns. Please refer to www.franklintempleton.com for more information about the portfolio, including objective, risks and investment process.

Client portfolios are managed based on investment instructions or advice provided by the following Franklin Templeton affiliated subadvisor: Western Asset Management Company. Management is implemented by FTPPG, the designated sub-advisor or, in the case of certain programs, the program sponsor or its designee.

Where FTPPG implements, Western Asset selects broker/dealers to execute trades; and certain Western Asset employees, acting as dual employees of FTPPG, may perform all or some of FTPPG’s implementation services. FTPPG and Western Asset are subsidiaries of Franklin Templeton.

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. Management and performance of individual accounts may vary for reasons that include the existence of different implementation practices and model requirements in different investment programs.

However, such information has not been verified, and may be different from the information included in documents and materials created by the sponsor firm in whose investment program a client participates. Some sponsor firms may require that these materials be preceded or accompanied by investment profiles or other documents or materials prepared by such sponsor firms, which will be provided upon a client’s request. data.title: Western Asset Gov/Corp Portfolios Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

  • Economic uncertainties triggered a flight to quality during the first quarter of 2025, driving U.S. Treasury yields lower and their prices higher. The economy continued to expand, while inflation remained higher than the Federal Reserve’s (“Fed”) 2% target. Meanwhile, President Trump’s plans for new reciprocal tariffs on U.S. imports led to concerns of rising inflation. All told, the overall U.S. bond market, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index, returned 2.78% during the first quarter. In contrast, the municipal (“muni”) market modestly declined over the quarter.

Quarterly Key Performance Drivers

Allocation

Security Selection

HELPED

Yield curve positioning

Higher quality securities

Bank/finance

HURT

Duration positioning

Overweight investment-grade corporate bonds
  • A short duration position detracted from returns.
  • Yield curve positioning was a slight positive for performance as the curve began to steepen.
  • An overweight to investment-grade corporate bonds was a modest detractor from returns.
  • Security selection contributed to performance, given the portfolio’s higher quality bias and overweight to bank/finance securities.

Outlook & Strategy

  • The disinflationary trend may be interrupted as tariffs and retaliatory actions are implemented, but we expect inflation to move lower again over the longer term. Monetary policy remains restrictive. We expect central banks will continue to cut rates modestly in 2025. The Fed remains well positioned to provide support if the U.S. economy falters. Public debt levels continue to rise and yield curves may steepen given concerns over fiscal policies globally.
  • We see pockets of opportunity in developed market rates in Europe, the UK and Australia. While the overall uncertainty in the market environment necessitates caution, we do see some longer-term value opportunities in emerging market local currency debt.
  • Spread sector fundamentals remain supportive, but valuations reflect those fundamentals and credit spreads persist at below historical averages. We continue to find opportunities within spread sectors and related securities, while remaining tactical.
  • Developed market duration provides useful diversification.

data.title: 2 High-Yield Dividend Aristocrats That Are On Sale
data.description:
Many things have changed in the markets since the President Trump took the Office. Most of the changes have a negative connotation in the context of asset allocation and shareholder returns.

For example:

  • Most of the equities are down (both growth and value).
  • Fixed income has declined as well.
  • The uncertainty levels are very high, which have forced some investor groups to completely pull out the capital from the markets (e.g., those that are about to retire and rely on harvesting portfolio gains).
  • The inflation problems are likely to return, but it is difficult to predict whether these effects will be transitory or with some long-lasting consequences.
  • The overall growth will most probably experience real pressures, which could potentially trigger elevated bankruptcies, especially in the weaker segments of the economy.

The only positive I see from all of this is that the valuations have come down a bit that has, among other things, lifted up the yields for both risk-on and risk-off assets.

In my opinion, the question is how we can capitalize on this positive nuance that has emerged as a consequence of the recent (and still to some extent ongoing) shock-therapy.

My approach is simple: remain biased towards non-cyclical businesses that can handle both inflation and weaker growth problems in a manner that does not impair their underlying value and the ability to keep distributing attractive dividends.

Also, an important disclosure here is that my investing approach is price-agnostic - i.e., it really does not matter for me how the prices evolve as long as the dividends are stable and growing at a rate that exceeds the inflation.

Here are two relatively high yield dividend aristocrats that I see being on sale.

#1 - Enterprise Products Partners, 7%

Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (NYSE:EPD) is, in my view, a true definition of durable income producing asset. It has distributed cash to its unitholders for 26 years in a row, recording no reductions in the process.

The recent market sell-off has also affected EPD, as the stock has lost around 9% over the past couple of weeks. As a result of this, we have the option to lock in EPD at a relatively decent yield of 7%.

Now, what are the key reasons why EPD has lost so much from its market cap.

There are two.

First, the consequences from the broader market dynamics that have led to capital outflows from equity-like products.

Second, and this is more specific, the energy prices have plunged, which, in turn, opens up many theoretical risks to a business that operates in the oil and/or nat gas space.

For instance, lower energy prices lead to reduced production and new investment amounts. If the economy really contracts, then the chances are very high that we will see a drop in demand for the energy.

For the upstream businesses it is, obviously, a huge shock.

For the midstream segment the situation is not that bad as irrespective of prices, new investments and changes in the production volumes, there will always be a need to move around the carbon molecules (both domestically and internationally into the export markets).

However, I would say that EPD is much better positioned than the rest of its midstream peers.

I will give you 5 arguments.

First, about 90% of EPD's income comes from fee-based (take or pay) contracts that provide downside protection on both the volume and price front. Plus, these contracts in most cases come with embedded CPI revenue escalators, which can advance the cash flows even if the volume/price floor is met.

Second, EPD has, arguably, one of the best balance sheets in the game. It has an upper investment grade capital structure with a weighted average debt yield of 4.7%. The latter aspect is quite important as it indicates an immaterial gap between the current IG financing yields and what has been carried by EPD (i.e., the cash flow pressures from debt refinancings are insignificant).

Third, EPD's distribution coverage is 1.7x, which gives plenty of margin of safety to absorb financial shocks without impairing the distribution durability/sustainability factor. Plus, the surplus undistributed cash flow can be nicely channeled into either new accretive CapEx projects or opportunistic buybacks without involving incremental debt.

Fourth, EPD plays across almost the whole midstream value chain. It has pipes, storage facilities, fractionation and processing. It introduces a decent lever of diversification, which comes in handy during times like these.

Fifth, I think that it is worth to appreciate the historical cash flow track record as a separate factor, which drives the investment case here. In my view, the chart below captures the entire essence of EPD very well. It just shows how durable the business model is. Even in times of totally collapsing and for a short period negative oil prices, EPD has managed to deliver stable cash flows.

In a nutshell, EPD is an easy choice for investors, who seek sustainable and above average current income streams.

#2 - Realty Income Corporation, 5.7%

Realty Income Corporation (NYSE:O) is another high-quality dividend aristocrat gem that has gone through a huge roller coaster ride since the long-term yield started to go in the unfavorable direction (from around October 2024). The past month and weeks have not been the greatest either, but, currently, the stock price has recovered from the dip. Yet, the stock is down by ~8% starting from October, last year.

In terms of the yield, what we, currently, can get is almost the highest level the O has provided over the past 10-year period. It is because the share price has remained somewhat stagnant, while the business has nicely registered a ~4% dividend 10-year CAGR.

Many of my investors have probably heard me (or read my articles) many times plotting a bull case on O. So, I will quickly outline the most important fundamental elements, which, in my view, form a compelling dividend case. Then I will touch upon an interesting nuance/advantage, which has not been discussed too much in the context of O's bull case.

So, the fundamentals that make O a "sleep-well-at-night" dividend investment are:

  • Upper investment grade balance sheet, which for REITs is more important than for MLPs (e.g., EPD). This is because REITs tend to assume more leverage, when doing M&As (i.e., buying properties). So, the notion of having an upper investment grade credit rating allows O to both avoid mitigate the unfavorable refinancing risks and be more competitive when buying growth properties.
  • Strongly diversified business model. While it is primarily a net-lease retail REIT, a tangible chunk of leases stems from data centers, gaming and industrial segments, which are, inherently, more defensive than conventional retail. Plus, we have to keep in mind that O is one of the largest REITs, which helps mitigate single-tenant concentration risk. Finally, O invests heavily in the UK and Europe that further enhances the diversification aspect.
  • O retains about 25% of its FFO generation in its books that just as in the case of EPD contributes to a solid margin of safety and a debt-free growth potential. Speaking of the FFO and the cash flows, a weighted average lease term of ~10 years with ~2% annual lease escalators ensure a stable growth factor that can come in handy when the M&A opportunities are put on pause.

Now, the aspect, which is rather unique about O (in a positive sense) is its exposure to the non-US markets. Here is a historical statistic showing how massively O has deployed capital into the EU markets. Plus, in the past couple of years, O has started to invest in the UK market as well. So, the total lease share that stems from non-US markets is a bit less than 20%.

There are a couple of positives from this, like the benefit of weakening USD versus GBP and EUR (i.e., the GBP and EUR cash flows converted to USD have now much bigger impact than before the tariff-saga). Furthermore, O can enjoy the benefit of tighter credit spread and lower all-in yields in the EU IG capital markets space.

The bottom line

Synchronized market drawdowns create opportunities for investors to buy high-quality at cheaper prices. For income investors, these cheaper prices mean higher entry yields, which is a huge benefit.

Granted, we have to be careful of not falling into value traps. We have to give some respect to the market and appreciate that it is still a future cash flow discounting machine that factors in new risks and opportunities.

However, as long the underlying business fundamentals are robust enough, relatively non-cyclical, and there is no aggressive risk taking from the management's side, we can really capitalize on these kinds of opportunities.

In this context, EPD and O are great picks that patient income investors can choose now to complement their current income streams.

data.title: It Will Be A Seesaw Moving Forward
data.description:
Yesterday was a snooze compared to what investors have had to endure over the past two weeks since the reciprocal tariff program was announced on “liberation day.” I think the relative calm has come because the stock, bond, and currency markets have been temporarily pacified by the numerous pivots, pauses, and exceptions to a tariff policy that would have raised the average levy on US imports from approximately 2.5% at the start of this year to a staggering 27%. At that rate, a deep consumer-led recession would be a foregone conclusion, which is why the S&P 500 narrowly dodged a bear market decline.

The index has now rallied more than 11% from its correction low of 4835, but further progress will depend on what I view as a seesaw with trade policy on one end and stock, bond, and currency markets on the other. If tariffs come down, risk asset prices will go up. If tariffs remain in place, stocks will drift lower, bond prices will decline and yields will rise, while the dollar will continue to weaken. The president has indicated he won’t change his mind on tariffs, but the multiple modifications to his policy suggest otherwise. For now, it looks like a decline to 4800 on the S&P 500 or an increase in the 10-year yield above 4.5% are the catalysts for another pivot, but eventually, it will be a deterioration in the high frequency economic data that forces him to pivot again.

Last week I surmised that we had 30 days to fix this because the longer our economy is stuck with the status quo of uncertainty, the more likely abysmal consumer and investor sentiment bleeds into the real economy. Even if tariffs are replaced with trade deals, the fear that they will stick has thrown supply chains in disarray, drawn forward some purchases, postponed others, while leaving consumers and business owners in a general state of discontent. The first indication that horrible sentiment is starting to impact activity was seen in last week’s gasoline usage numbers, which showed a year-over-year decline of 2.8% in the four-week moving average. This is a real-time pulse on consumer discretionary spending.

Additionally, the longer it takes for risk asset prices to recover, the more likely a negative wealth effect could take hold, as the top 10% of households by income own the majority of stock market wealth and account for 50% of overall consumer spending. Yesterday, the S&P 500 formed a discouraging “death cross,” whereby the 50-day moving average fell below the 200-day moving average. It is not as ominous as it seems, though, despite suggesting a loss of near-term price momentum because the index has been higher one year later in 80% of the prior 20 occurrences. I think it will be higher than yesterday’s close of 5,596 one year from now as well.

I think the seesaw analogy is appropriate because markets will ultimately force the president to back down from the tariff policies that very few support in the days and weeks ahead. The loss of wealth from declining stock prices, higher borrowing costs from lower bond prices, and the increasing probability of a painful recession will only serve to weaken his base of support.

If that process starts sooner rather than later, investors can start focusing on multiple tailwinds, the most important of which is monetary policy easing by the Fed to loosen financial conditions. The consensus is now expecting four more rate cuts from the central bank this year, lowering the benchmark rate from its current 4.25-4.5% range to 3.25-3.5%. I still see three rate cuts, as I have since the beginning of this year, because I think the Fed will still be dealing with the unknown impact of a more modest version of the current tariff program. Regardless, this is a tailwind for risk asset prices during the second half of this year.

data.title: ASML: €570/Share Is As Cheap As It Gets--I'm Buying Q1 Earnings Dip
data.description:
Tariff-related uncertainty is not only making stock-picking incredibly hard, with stocks trading on politics and news instead of fundamentals, but it's clouding the outlook for European crown jewel ASML Holding (NASDAQ:ASML).

ASML reported its Q1 earnings this morning, and Amsterdam-domiciled stock has fallen 5% as the company reported solid earnings but once again missed order expectations as US-induced import tariffs are clouding the demand for chip-making equipment.

While AI-related demand, particularly for its high-end EUV chip-making machines, was resilient, ASML has seen its DUV sales weakening as demand for smartphones, appliances, and electronics faced a period of consolidation, alongside chip export restrictions to China.

While I previously argued tariff may actually be a tailwind for ASML's product line as more production is moved to the US, increasing demand for its equipment, the short-term uncertainty with some customers lowering their CAPEX is prompting the company to be cautious, now guiding to land 2025 at the lower end of its guidance.

Previous Coverage

Two months ago, I wrote an article titled "ASML: The Turnaround Is Now Confirmed," rating the stock "Strong Buy" amidst strong AI-related demand, recovery in sight for the lower-end of chip-making, and forward-twelve-month valuation at 25x its earnings.

Since then, the stock has pulled back 5%, yet the performance of the broader market (SP500) has been worse, pulling as much as 10%. Semiconductor stocks are inherently volatile, and it's encouraging to see that ASML's volatility is, in fact, lower than that of the US stock market, with participants starting to understand how cheap, in fact, ASML's shares are. If you are a US-based investor and buy EU-domiciled or ADR shares, you will also benefit from the strengthening EUR/USD.

In my last coverage, I expected US/Dutch government export restrictions to have a minimal impact on ASML's demand. New fabs planned in the US and Europe would offset the decline in China-driven sales despite the delays in fab development due to a limited skilled workforce and Intel (INTC) challenges. Don't forget, ASML can recognize its revenue only once its equipment is delivered to the customer.

ASML's management was cautiously optimistic, as weak guidance pointed to sufficient chip-making capacity, but recovery in consumer spending, particularly in PC and smartphone sales, would likely result in a new round of CAPEX investment cycle, with recovery in H2 2025 - H1 2026

Today, we have new clouds on the horizon, and it's time to provide an update to my investment thesis.

Q1 Earnings

ASML's Q1 earnings were solid yet missed the estimates on the top line and beat on the bottom.

ASML reported Net Sales of €7.74B, matching the company's previous guidance, but analysts were expecting €7.8B amidst the increased AI-related spending.

The Gross margin, a metric I watch closely to see if ASML retains its monopoly-like status with industry-leading pricing power, landed at 54%, above the company's guidance, the highest in the last 5 quarters, helped by a favorable product mix with 56% EUV sales, compared to 42% in Q4 2024.

As a result, the bottom line, net income, landed at €2.36B or €6.00 EPS, above analyst expectations of €2.3B.

In Q1, ASML shipped its first High NA system, the next generation of extreme ultraviolet lithography designed for cutting-end chip-making. The average sticker price is €400M per unit, double the cost of previous low-NA EUV equipment.

Q1 saw ASML selling 77 lithography systems, with an average price of €100M, significantly fewer systems compared to Q2 sales of 132 systems, but at a lower price of €70M per unit.

The product mix is heavily influenced by geographical exposure, as you can see below on the right, as each market is purchasing different systems influenced by the export restrictions.

You might be asking yourself, if earnings were solid, why is the stock dropping 5%?

The answer is net bookings.

ASML's net bookings—a key KPI to track order demand—in Q1 came at €3.94B, falling short of analysts' estimates of €4.89B, with ASML's CEO confirming the challenges but still expecting 2025-2026 to be growth years:

"Our conversations so far with customers support our expectation that 2025 and 2026 will be growth years. However, the recent tariff announcements have increased uncertainty in the macro environment and the situation will remain dynamic for a while. As previously shared, artificial intelligence continues to be the primary growth driver in our industry. It has created a shift in the market dynamics that benefits some customers more than others, contributing to both upside potential and downside risks as reflected in our 2025 revenue range.

Net Bookings are a very volatile indicator of ASML's workload, as you can judge for yourself from the graph below (the blue columns). Given the current backlog worth €35B, ASML's business remains safe and sound. Each quarter, the business fulfills around €6-8B of its backlog, and ASML is fully booked for another five quarters.

The focus of investors here is on the potential damage tariffs may inflict on ASML. The temporary weakness that we are seeing is, in my opinion, not driven by the lack of demand for ASML's machines but by the uncertainty, with businesses like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), Intel pausing their CAPEX spending, seeking more clarity on how the back-and-forth import duties will play out and if indeed relocating production to the US is the only viable option.

Initially, Trump announced a 32% tariff on Taiwanese goods, where most advanced chips are produced, just to be revised to 10% for the next 90 days.

ASML's revenue exposure to the US in Q1 was 16% of sales and in Q4 2024 28%, but I expect that with the new fabs coming online, more of ASML's equipment will be heading to the US, and a 10% tariff won't damage their demand.

Let's be honest: whether with a 10% or 25% tariff, a cutting-edge chipmaker won't refuse the best-in-class technology if there is no other viable alternative.

ASML's guidance now points to net sales of €7.2-7.7B in Q2, with the gross margin expected to continue trending above 50%.

The management also doubled down on their long-term guidance, expecting full-year 2025 sales to land on the lower end of the €30-40B range, marking 10-15% growth from full-year 2024 sales of €28.2B.

As European-based companies don't pay a regular, fixed quarterly dividend, ASML now intends to pay a full-year 2024 dividend of €6.4/share. Considering the three previous dividends of €1.52 paid in 2024, the final dividend in 2025 will be €1.84 if approved during the Annual General Meeting.

Previously, I criticized ASML for its inefficient buyback strategy. The business didn't buy back any of its shares during the weakness we saw in Q4 2024. Finally, we can see that the company repurchased €2.7B worth of shares under the 2022-2025 buyback program.

Yet, given the weakness, with shares now trading in deep value territory at ~570EUR/share, I would appreciate a more aggressive buyback strategy.

Valuation & Forward Growth

ASML's shares are currently valued at a Blended P/E of 31.7x, below their 15Y normalized P/E of 33.2.

If we, for instance, consider the FY25 forecasted EPS of $25.38, the shares are on a 9-month forward basis, trading at ~27x forward P/E.

I'll let you make up your own mind, but for me, a business that is projected to grow its EPS at a rate of 20-30% annually for the next few years, this seems far too cheap.

We don't speak about "any" business but monopoly-like players with cutting-edge technology.

Yes, ASML's business is highly cyclical, even though Installed Base Management, that are less cyclical are increasly contributing more to the overall sales, selling a low volume, 50-150 pieces of equipment per quarter, is seen as risk.

FactSet, is now forecasting the following EPS growth, which is actuallyan improvement from my last coverage:

  • FY2025: EPS of $25.38E, 27% YoY growth
  • FY2026: EPS of $30.66E, 21% YoY growth
  • FY2027: EPS of $36.53E, 19% YoY growth

I see ASML's valuation as a rare opportunity to buy the best-in-class lithography equipment maker. Myself, I am collecting the low-hanging fruit, buying more shares below €600/share, setting the stage for future outperformance as the secular tailwinds of increased digitalization are unlikely to fade.

In my view, investors could earn up to 20-25% annual ROR if we have more clarity on the tariff-related uncertainty and the sentiment improves, pushing the stock towards its 15Y average P/E of 33.2x.

Investor's Takeaway

ASML's Q1 earnings sent the stock lower by 5%, even though the results were surprisingly good.

The issue is that ASML's stock is not trading based on its reported earnings but based on its net bookings and the outlook, which is currently overshadowed by the US-induced import tariffs, with key customers delaying their CAPEX spending, seeking more clarity.

If tariffs stay, cutting-edge chip manufacturers are unlikely not to purchase ASML's chipmaking equipment because of a 10-25% tariff, but consumer demand could be impacted.

ASML's management doubled down on its Q2 and full-year 2025 guidance despite the challenges, and I see the shares as undervalued, increasing my exposure at ~570EUR/share.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Solaris Energy Infrastructure: Speculative Play On Mobile Power
data.description:
Introduction

It's been a while since we put out a note on this legacy frac patch rental equipment supplier. Solaris Energy, (NYSE:SEI) made a sharp left turn last summer with its acquisition of Mobile Energy Rentals, LLC. The stock just exploded upward on the release of the Q-3 earnings report, in a parabolic move that tripled the stock price at its peak. Assisted likely by a lot of happy talk about the growth potential of their new business. Alas, that trajectory was not to be maintained, as investors took stock on some white space on their power calendar. On the release of earnings on Feb, 20th, aided by energy market concerns, it began a steep descent toward it point of origin.

We aren't quite there yet, but I don't expect the market is going to pay 30X forward earnings to own this company, so further declines may be in the offing. And, that could spell opportunity!

Analysts remain bullish with a strong buy rating on the stock with price targets of $44.00-$53.00 that would make the current entry point a pretty good one. In their most recent quarterly release, Q-4, 2024 they beat estimates by a nickel, and the forecast for the first half is for flat EPS of $0.14. Not especially inspiring.

In this review, we will touch on the business case for portable and modular power to provide some context for sniffing around this oilfield stalwart at current levels.

The mobile/modular power business case

Stories about the power demand for AI data centers are legion by now. It's a pretty well-understood scenario. What is less well understood is the lack of grid capacity in place to supply the power they will need to host their AI offerings. It's massive. But the gap between the ability to throw up a building, fill it with servers, and the ability of utilities to run power to them is months to years in some cases.

Something that's hard to assess accurately right now, but is likely to increase power demands exponentially, is the reshoring of manufacturing as companies reposition themselves to compete in the new tariff environment. The "bridge" between the utilities and these new demands for power are the mobile/modular solutions provided by SEI. CEO Bill Zartler lays out the case in the call-

Today's power applications are getting larger and more numerous coupled with customers recognizing the longer-term nature of their requirements that has driven this nearly 10-fold increase of our business to 1.4 gigawatts. A single data center today can require well over a gigawatt of power, and some oilfield and other microgrids are approaching 100 megawatts in power demand.

Our integrated, behind-the-meter power solution is well suited to satisfy those needs. The growing need for power is being driven by the electrification of everything, the domestic reshoring of manufacturing, and the growing quantity and scale of data centers. Demand for power has outpaced investment in infrastructure, creating a significant opportunity for our Power Solutions segment. This has resulted in extended grid interconnection wait times, in turn driving a greater need for bridge and permanent behind-the-meter long-term power solutions, which we are well positioned to supply.

For many customers, that bridge time frame is extending, thus behind-the-meter power is evolving toward more permanent power which will supplement the grid by helping manage complex loads, providing redundancy and potentially even improving grid resilience. The notion of "bring your own power" is becoming a requirement for many industrial applications.

The thesis for SEI

We know SEI fairly well as a provider of sand silos and "last mile" infrastructure related to fracking. This is highly responsive to the rig/frac spread count, and has been a collapsing market over the last couple of years. Further, there is no particular moat in this space, as sand and pumping equipment providers have developed competitive solutions of their own. It's difficult to imagine a growth case scenario for SEI in this space that would match the rate seen in the late teens. It's a good business, but not one that the market finds particularly exciting.

That's certainly not the case for the "behind the meter," mobile power business, as the slide above stipulates. The company currently has about half its power footprint contracted and has recently inked a 500 MW long-term deal with an undisclosed customer. As noted in the slide above, SEI expects Power Solutions to contribute about 80% in years to come.

Kyle Ramachandran - President discusses the specifics of this deal-

We've reached an agreement with this customer to provide a minimum of 500 megawatts for an initial tenor of six years for a new data center. And we are in the process of finalizing details of a joint venture arrangement with our customer to support this investment.

This joint venture structure appeals to us for many reasons. It aligns our interest with those of our customers. It also demonstrates the long-term nature of the customer's commitment to behind-the-meter power given their desire to retain partial ownership in these assets. And lastly, it demonstrates the confidence they have in Solaris as a partner that has a proven ability to execute over the long term.

Under the proposed structure, we would own 50.1% of the assets and we'll operate and manage the equipment on behalf of the JV. We expect that the partnership will pursue debt financing directly at the JV level to fund a significant portion of the purchase of the assets. For reporting purposes, we expect to consolidate the financial results of the full partnership with our customers' equity portion of earnings reported as non-controlling interest.

The impact of this partnership on our fleet would result in a net owned fleet of approximately 1,100 megawatts, out of the total owned and operated fleet of 1,400 megawatts. Of this capacity, roughly 450 megawatts are available for future contracting with customers for deliveries beginning in the second half of 2026. The pace and trajectory of our ongoing commercial discussion gives us confidence that we will contract the remaining capacity.

My thoughts. The JV structure seems like a pretty slick deal for SEI. It minimizes the upfront capex required and keeps them from destroying their balance sheet with growth capex. If they can continue with this model, a lot of the debt risk potential can be minimized.

Q-4, 2024 and Guidance

During the fourth quarter, Solaris generated total revenue of $96 million, which reflected a 28% increase from the prior quarter due to a full quarter contribution from Solaris Power Solutions. Adjusted EBITDA of $37 million represented a 68% increase from the prior quarter. Solaris Power Solutions contributed more than 50% of the adjusted EBITDA mix and is on track to contribute nearly 80% of earnings after the on-order fleet is deployed.

During the fourth quarter, SEI earned revenue on an average of approximately 260 megawatts in Solaris Power Solutions. For the first quarter of 2025, the company is increasing activity guidance, as measured by average megawatts earning revenue, by 20% to 360 megawatts. For the second quarter, they expect average megawatts on revenue to increase by 17% to approximately 420 megawatts.

Solaris Logistics Solutions segment, expects their fully utilized systems to grow over 15% to approximately 90 to 95 systems and remain there for the first half of the year. Profits are expected to be higher in this segment as well.

Guidance for Q-1, 2025

The EBITDA forecast is between $44 million and $48 million in Q1, and adjusted EBITDA between $50 million and $55 million for Q2.

SEI has just authorized their 26th consecutive dividend of $0.12 per share, paid on March 21st to holders of record as of March 11, 2025. At their latest share count, this should equate to a little more than $8 million.

Solaris holds $307 mm of long-term debt in the form of a Term Loan. Quarterly payments of 1.5% of the balance are due, with the outstanding amounts maturing September, 11, 2029. SEI also has a $75 mm revolver with no draws as of the annual report.

SEI has the cash flow at current levels to pay their indebtedness, maintain and increase their dividend. Funding growth capex for 2025 will come from asset sales, new debt, or printing stock.

Source

Risks

Power generation is a new business for SEI, precipitated by their $300 mm acquisition of MER last year. The initial results have been good, but they have a lot of uncontracted capacity and were a little vague as to when it might be rented out on long-term contracts.

Question from Analyst, Derrick Whitfield-

First, regarding the 450 megawatts of uncontracted capacity, how aggressively will you look to market that given the tightness in the market dynamics for behind-the-meter solutions?

Reply from CEO, Bill Zartler-

I mean, we're in a lot of -- several active discussions, and I don't think there's a need to get overly impatient with it as we see it and as we're putting money down toward the acquisition of that, we're not out a lot of cash at this point relative to the timing of doing that.

Hmmm.

The real growth opportunities take them out of their oilfield comfort zone, and power generation is becoming a crowded market.

The company recently ordered 700 MW of new generating equipment with capital commitments that appear to unfunded by anything other than printing stock, floating new debt. They discuss the risk below.

Total purchase commitments related to our power generation fleet growth program amounted to $788.8 million, consisting of $239.7 million outstanding as of December 31, 2024, and $549.1 million entered into in 2025. We estimate that approximately $693.8 million will be fulfilled in 2025, and approximately $95.0 million will be fulfilled in 2026. While these commitments are aligned with our growth strategy, they are cancellable, though subject to significant termination penalties ranging from 5% to 90% of the purchase price, depending on the timing of the cancellation. If we are unable to secure appropriate financing, we can manage the cancellation penalties, ensuring that we have the financial capacity to absorb such costs, as necessary.

Long term-Undefined, gas fired generators could face risks from the deployment of Small Modular Nuclear Reactors-SMNR. These are being developed by a number of companies and are probably less than a couple of years from field deployment.

Your takeaway

SEI presents an attractive growth prospect. The company is trading at about 12.6X EV/EBITDA, which puts them in the middle of the mobile power generating cohort. The company pays a 2.75% yielding dividend.

If an investor has a substantial appetite for risk to achieve outsized gains in the success case for the company, a bet on SEI at or slightly below current levels might be attractive.

With the fairly flat earnings forecast, the smart play might be to put them on the watch list for a slightly more advantageous entry point-5 or 10% below current levels. SEI reports Q-1 on April, 28th after the market close.

data.title: Everest Group: The Trouble With Growing Too Fast
data.description:
Everest Group (NYSE:EG), the property casualty insurer and reinsurer based in Bermuda, looks cheap at $350 relative to both potential earnings per share (around $40 or $50, on a normal year) and book value (about 1.1x).

Looks may be deceiving, however, as the company went through a difficult 2024 that included a very large reserve revision and the (perhaps related) change of their CEO.

There is potential, given the size of the operation and its built-in distribution network. Everest is a key player in the reinsurance market, their position within reinsurance brokerage is well entrenched. They also deal directly with ceding companies, enhancing their distribution capacity.

The company is not at risk of going under, I rate it "sell" solely because there is too much uncertainty (still) around reserving discipline and therefore too much potential for underperformance as they work through those problems. But I would not short it either.

This is a classic tale of well-intentioned but misplaced KPI’s leading into behavior that ends up hurting performance in the long term. I will get to that, but first, a quick review of the business.

Everest in the market

Everest has achieved impressive growth in Gross Written Premiums (GWP), the equivalent of "revenue" for the insurance industry. They found growth in both, their bread and butter reinsurance operation and their smaller, but also fast-growing, insurance operation.

For re-insurance:

For insurance:

As charts above show, premiums essentially doubled for both within a five-year period. 15% CAGR for reinsurance and 14% for insurance. It is true that there was a hard market though this half-decade, but still, premium volume stood at 1.3x equity, a noteworthy increase from 2019, where this ratio was 1:1.

The problem with growth

Growth in the insurance industry, similar to banking, is not hard to find. In banking, where the business is literally giving money away (by placing loans), very fast growth can be sustained for a while without using too much imagination.

In insurance and reinsurance, similar dynamics are at play. A premium is received up-front and should be sized to cover expected losses. The operative word is "should". The real cost of servicing policies is only known later – sometimes much later – as some losses develop years after the policy was issued and premiums were paid.

Loss estimates are just that, estimates, and they can be misjudged in either direction. There are issues on both sides of the coin. A “too-conservative (‘high’)” reserve approach can lead to higher pricing on new policies and therefore loss of market share. On the other hand, a “too-aggressive (‘low’)” reserving approach will lead to surprise losses further down, and to current policies also being mis-priced, leading to potential further revisions down the road.

This spiraling dynamic is one reason Warren Buffett warns against setting premium volume targets as a metric for rewarding management. Insurance markets are cyclical, so is capital committed to it, and therefore underwriting discipline can only be maintained if premium levels are allowed to increase and decrease in response to prevailing competitive conditions.

These were the goals Everest Group set for itself during their 2021 investor day:

It is worth remembering that the insurance market was becoming very favorable for insurers (the opposite for policy buyers), after Covid and this likely influenced their aggressive GPW growth targets (up to 22% CAGR for insurance!)... essentially a doubling in three years at the top end.

Remember the premium growth charts in the preceding section (a CAGR at the top end of the Group target of 15%). Management did their job, responded to their incentives (which were repeated in the more recent investor day) and delivered the growth at the top end of guidance.

But at what cost? We would soon find out.

Coming home to roost

Everest closed 2024 with a meaningful revision to past reserves. The prior-year reserve development (PYD) charge was $1.7B pre-tax.

For context, below is the 1-in-100-year PML (probable maximum loss) on their policies:

A loss of 10% percent of equity equals $1.4B. Thus, the hit to earnings is roughly equivalent to the maximum loss expected in a 1-in-a-100-year earthquake in California. That is a very large loss indeed.

Unlike catastrophes that large, which lead to immediate premium increases stemming from the diminished capital available, a “reserve true-up” loss affects only the insurer, with no offsetting industry impacts.

Coming to the rescue were improved investment results - driven by an increased interest income - of $1.9B, which doubled the levels from three years back. The year ended in the black, although profits were roughly half of those in 2023.

The investment portfolio

As noted, the level of investment income is improving. It used to be around half of where it was in 2024 just three years ago. What drives this change is the gradual maturity of low coupon bonds with higher coupon bonds. Their portfolio duration is roughly 3 years (matching estimated reserve liability duration), with tenors averaging 4 years.

As such, there may still be a year or so left of bond substitution, meaning interest income could raise a little bit this year as well. There is still some juice left to squeeze. Even if rates come down somewhat, new coupon rates are already mostly locked in, defending interest income for the near term.

The size of the portfolio is larger than one may expect on $14B in equity. Due to large float, the investments have surpassed $41B. About 86% of which is invested in highly rated fixed income. The rest, an amount management considers in excess of what is required to meet losses (and thus, “equity funded”), is invested in private equity partnerships and to a lesser degree in public stocks.

Investment duties are largely farmed out to asset managers and the results are in line with what investors should expect and also within regulatory parameters.

Is the price right?

After largely keeping up with peers, the stock performance started to lag during 2024. The big write-down was announced in Q4. However, the underperformance started earlier. Perhaps their decision to issue additional stock (for a small amount at around $360) in 2023 started to irk investors somewhat. The stock never moved much above that level and is there still today.

Commensurate with the stock lagging peers, the dividend yield has improved. The company has been a consistent dividend payer and has managed to fund it even during adverse cat loss years, a feather in their cap.

Risks

I don’t expect EG to outperform either peers or the market in the next few years. Apart from increased losses already booked, another type of loss seems to be investor trust. And that trust is hard to earn back.

This could be wrong, of course. Investing requires assessment of probabilities, and there is a chance that the market is soothed quickly, and the multiples re-rate. Especially if those very same underwriting results turn decidedly positive in the short term.

Debt is not an issue, their roughly $2B is low coupon with maturities in excess of 20 years from now. Debt will not cause any existential risks to the company, even in the case of potential future ratings downgrades.

Wrapping it up

After what looks like a kitchen-sink approach to fixing their reserves, the future may be brighter for Everest Group. A new leader always brings new hope (tempered in this case by the fact the choice was an insider). I wish that to be the case, that better days are ahead, as having one more candidate re-join the investable universe is better than not.

But still, I worry. The size of the loss development is hard to swallow. It should mean they did more than they absolutely had to (in fact, they signaled a +$400 million cushion over actuarial results), but it could also mean something else.

It may mean there are some potentially unearthed skeletons in the reserve closet. Clearly, underwriting discipline was relaxed in pursuit of premium dollars, the consequences of that can be felt long into the future in their long-tail casualty lines.

Their marginal reorganization, creating the “other” segment (the “bad bank” approach) for runoff lines of business, may be an attempt to further clear out the book through there.

Either way, better alternatives exist. Both CB and MKL have demonstrated much stronger underwriting track records and capital allocation discipline. I would love to one day add back EG to the portfolio, but there is no rush. Time is the ultimate revealer of truth - I can wait.
